narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated an 89% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong points like an LSAT score slightly above the typical 36.7 average and upper-middle family income, which is around what most applicants have, along with being a full-time male student from a White background—demographics that match the majority in our dataset. However, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 sits a bit below the average 3.21, and that's part of what raised concerns. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of 5.71, signaling early academic struggles compared to peers. Right after that in influence came your LSAT score, which, despite being solid, didn't offset other risks enough in the model's eyes. Your Year 3 decile of 1, again well under the 5.53 average, ranked third in sway and reinforced worries about consistent performance. Family income came next, as upper-middle status didn't provide the extra buffer some higher brackets do for bar success. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely, nudging the odds higher for failure due to being under average. Less influential but still contributing were your racial background and gender, which align with typical successful students but couldn't counterbalance the academics. Full-time status had the smallest impact, as it's the norm and didn't shift things much. Overall, your low decile rankings in Years 1 and 3 stood out most against typical students who pass, painting a picture of academic hurdles the model links strongly to bar failure. To improve your future chances, focus on boosting your grades to climb those school deciles—dedicated study habits and tutoring could make a real difference there. Retaking the LSAT for an even higher score might help too, and building a stronger post-undergrad record, like through additional coursework, could lift your GPA profile over time. With targeted effort on academics, you could shift this prediction in your favor for the next application cycle.
